topic: diurnal temperature range, slow ripening, and aromatic freshness

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Regions with a large diurnal temperature range - where warm days promote sugar accumulation but cool nights slow respiration - create a distinctive ripening pattern.

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: The warm daytime temperatures allow photosynthesis and sugar development to proceed at pace, while cool overnight temperatures slow the respiratory breakdown of tartaric and malic acid. This preserves natural grape acidity and allows aromatic compounds to develop and persist in the grape skins without heat degradation.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: Wines from high-diurnal-range sites retain higher acidity, show pronounced and precise aromatic character, and often display a freshness or vibrancy that distinguishes them from wines of similar sugar ripeness grown in uniformly warm climates.

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: A large diurnal range acts as a quality-supporting factor: warm days develop flavour and sugar while cool nights preserve acidity and aromatic compounds. Wines from these sites therefore tend to show higher acidity and more defined, fresh aromatics than wines from isothermal warm climates at the same ripeness level.

sat_relevance: Useful in SAT when explaining high acidity combined with ripe fruit or good aromatic definition. Diurnal range supports the coexistence of ripeness and freshness in the same wine.

_meta.source_note: Inferred formative model - not derived from official WSET mark scheme or syllabus document
